_meta.title: Phase Capstone — Refactor a Real OSS Repo + ADR Bundle

questions.question: You inherit a 200-line god class that handles orders, accesses the database directly, sends emails, and calls an inventory API. What should you do FIRST before starting any refactoring?
options: Rewrite the class from scratch using hexagonal architecture
Write characterization tests that pin the current behavior
Write an ADR proposing the hexagonal architecture change
Extract the database calls into a separate module
questions.explanation: Refactoring mechanics (L06) require tests before any changes. Characterization tests capture what the code currently does, creating a safety net. Only then can you refactor with confidence. Writing the ADR comes next, and actual code changes follow.

questions.question: You're choosing an open-source project for this capstone. Which quality makes it a GOOD candidate?
options: It has comprehensive tests and clean architecture — you can learn by reading
It has some technical debt and coupling but is under 3,000 LOC — you can hold it in your head and make meaningful changes
It's a large popular framework — you can make a meaningful contribution
It uses the newest JavaScript framework — you can learn modern patterns
questions.explanation: The capstone goal is to practice reading a real codebase (L21), identifying debt (L19), and refactoring. A project under 3K LOC with real debt is ideal — large enough to have real problems, small enough to understand and change. A clean project offers nothing to refactor; a huge project can't be held in working memory.

questions.question: In the capstone, why do ADRs come BEFORE the refactoring (not after)?
options: Because architecture decisions must always be approved by a committee before implementation
Because ADRs capture the reasoning and context at the time of decision, preventing retroactive justification, and ensuring each refactoring step has a clear purpose
Because writing ADRs after refactoring is impossible — you can't remember what you changed
Because ADRs are required by law in some jurisdictions
questions.explanation: ADRs (L20) document the context, decision, and consequences at the moment of choice. Writing them before refactoring ensures you capture WHY you're making the change, not a post-hoc justification. Each refactoring step references its ADR, creating a traceable history from decision to implementation.

questions.question: After refactoring OrderService into hexagonal architecture with CQRS and events, a new requirement arrives: add SMS notifications alongside email. What does the refactored architecture let you do?
options: Add an SmsNotificationAdapter that implements NotificationPort and subscribe it to OrderCreatedEvent — no changes to domain or command logic
Modify OrderCommandService to call the SMS service alongside the email service
Add SMS logic to the existing SmtpNotificationAdapter and update its configuration
Create a new OrderServiceWithSms class that extends the original OrderService
questions.explanation: This is the Open/Closed Principle (L03) realized through hexagonal architecture (L09) and events (L10). New notification channels are added by creating a new adapter (implements NotificationPort) and subscribing it to OrderCreatedEvent. The domain and command service don't change at all — they're closed for modification but open for extension.

questions.question: Which capstone stage connects MOST directly to Lesson 19 (Technical Debt)?
options: Stage 1: Pick a project — choosing which project to refactor
Stage 3: Identify debt and smells — cataloging technical debt using the debt quadrant and mapping smells to lesson concepts
Stage 5: Refactor using SOLID — applying Single Responsibility and Dependency Inversion
Stage 7: Document via ADRs and commits — recording architectural decisions
questions.explanation: Stage 3 directly applies L19's debt framework: categorizing debt by quadrant (reckless/prudent × deliberate/inadvertent), assessing severity, and mapping each smell to a remediation lesson. While other stages use concepts from L19 indirectly, Stage 3 is the debt analysis itself.

questions.question: Your refactoring sequence is: (1) extract pricing method, (2) introduce Repository port, (3) move DB calls to adapter, (4) extract OrderPricingService, (5) add OrderCreatedEvent. Why must step 2 come before step 3?
options: Because the repository adapter needs a port to implement — you can't implement an interface that doesn't exist yet
Because extracting a method always requires an interface first
Because dependency injection requires interfaces, and step 3 uses dependency injection
The order doesn't matter — steps 2 and 3 can be done in either order
questions.explanation: In hexagonal architecture (L09), the port (interface) must exist before the adapter (implementation). You can't create an InMemoryOrderRepository that implements OrderRepository if the OrderRepository interface hasn't been defined yet. This follows Dependency Inversion (L03) — define the abstraction first, then implement it.
